items.q: What is the difference between the area and the perimeter of a shape?
items.correct: Perimeter is the distance around the edge; area is the space inside, measured in square units.
items.misconception: Area and perimeter are the same measurement.
items.random_wrong: Perimeter is always larger than area.
items.why: Students confuse the boundary length (perimeter) with the space enclosed (area).

items.q: If the radius of a circle doubles, what happens to its area?
items.correct: The area becomes four times as large, because area depends on the radius squared.
items.why: Learners apply linear scaling, forgetting that area scales with the square of the radius.

items.q: What units are used to measure volume?
items.correct: Cubic units, such as cubic centimetres (cm^3).
items.why: Students confuse the units of volume (cubic) with those of area (square) or length (linear).

items.q: The distance all the way around the edge of a circle is called its:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Circumference. “Area” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The area of a rectangle is found by:
items.why: It's Multiplying length by width. “Adding length and width” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The volume of a cuboid is calculated as:
items.why: Correct answer: length x width x height. “length x width” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The area of a circle of radius r is given by:
items.why: Correct answer: pi x r squared. “2 x pi x r” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A rectangle is 8 cm long and 5 cm wide. What is its area?
items.why: Exact answer: 40 cm^2. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
